title: Quantum footprints of symplectic rigidity
speaker: Leonid Polterovich
abstract: We discuss interactions between quantum mechanics and symplectic topology including a link between symplectic displacement energy, a fundamental notion of symplectic dynamics, and the quantum speed limit, a universal constraint on the speed of quantum-mechanical processes. Joint work with Laurent Charles.

proof_stages.label: Quantum states, fidelity, and phase-space shadows
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates the search for quantum traces of symplectic rigidity, sets up finite-dimensional quantum mechanics, defines fidelity of states, and interprets states through Husimi-type measures on the classical phase space.

proof_stages.label: Dislocation and the universal quantum speed limit
proof_stages.summary: A time-dependent Hermitian Hamiltonian generates Schrödinger evolution, a state is called dislocated when its fidelity with its evolved image is small, and the universal quantum speed limit gives a lower bound of order hbar for such dislocation.

proof_stages.label: Hofer displacement energy versus measure flexibility
proof_stages.summary: The classical side is recalled through Hofer displacement energy, whose positivity for open sets expresses symplectic rigidity, while a contrasting measure-theoretic flexibility result allows almost-displacement of sets of volume less than half by arbitrarily small energy.

proof_stages.label: Berezin-Toeplitz quantization of observables and states
proof_stages.summary: For a quantizable closed Kähler manifold, observables are quantized as Toeplitz operators on holomorphic sections, coherent-state projectors provide the geometric kernel, and classical probability measures are quantized by integrating these projectors.

proof_stages.label: Semiclassical equivalence of displacement and dislocation
proof_stages.summary: The main results with Laurent Charles state that classical displacement of the support gives rapid quantum dislocation, while sufficiently rapid semiclassical dislocation by quantized classical Hamiltonians forces classical displacement and hence energy at least the displacement energy.

proof_stages.label: Remainder bounds and small-scale limits of quantization
proof_stages.summary: The Berezin-Toeplitz norm, product, commutator, and trace correspondence laws are recalled with derivative-dependent remainders, showing how dislocation results extend to subconstant scales and why ideal quantization at the sqrt(hbar) scale is obstructed.

proof_stages.label: Noise localization and Poisson-bracket cover invariants
proof_stages.summary: A second quantum footprint is sketched: quantizing partitions of unity for an open cover creates unavoidable measurement noise controlled by commutators, leading to Poisson-bracket invariants of covers and conjectural bounds involving displacement energy.

examples.name: Pure states as vectors/projectors
examples.note: Pure states are used to make the abstract fidelity formula concrete, since for unit vectors it becomes the absolute value of the Hermitian inner product and orthogonality means zero fidelity.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker computes fidelity for pure states as the absolute Hermitian product and uses the projective-space geometry of orthogonal vectors to explain the speed-limit bound.

passes.reason: A pure state is a singular rank-one/minimal-rank density matrix and an extreme point of the convex state space, used to illustrate fidelity.
Pure states are rank-one/minimal-rank density matrices and extreme points of the convex quantum state space, used here only to illustrate fidelity.
Rank-one pure states are minimal-rank boundary/extreme points of the density-matrix convex set, used here to illustrate fidelity rather than refute a claim.
Pure states are rank-one density matrices, i.e. extreme points of the convex state space, and are used illustratively rather than adversarially.

Pure states are rank-one/minimal-rank extreme points of the convex state space, used only to illustrate fidelity.

examples.name: Almost displacing a ball by tiny pieces
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains the measure-flexibility statement for a ball by approximating it with many small cubes and moving them one by one with very small Hofer energy.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explains how to approximate a ball by a grid of small cubes and displace the tiny pieces one by one with energy of order the small radius squared.

passes.reason: The ball is a highly regular symmetric domain, and its almost-displacement by tiny pieces is used to show measure-theoretic symplectic rigidity fails.
The round ball is structurally symmetric, and its almost-displacement by arbitrarily tiny-energy pieces is used to refute a measure-theoretic rigidity analogue.
The ball is used in a fragmented almost-displacement construction to show that a measure-theoretic version of symplectic rigidity fails.

The tiny-piece almost-displacement construction is used to defeat naive measure-theoretic symplectic rigidity, while the construction itself is not a sporadic, extremal, or maximally symmetric object.
the nearly displaced ball is used to show measure-theoretic symplectic rigidity fails, while the ball itself is the standard highly symmetric domain
The round ball fragmented into tiny regular pieces is used to show almost-displacement with arbitrarily small energy, refuting measure-theoretic rigidity.
The tiny-piece near-displacement is a limiting zero-energy flexibility construction used to defeat measure-theoretic symplectic rigidity.
The tiny-piece limiting construction gives arbitrarily small-energy almost displacement, showing measure-theoretic symplectic rigidity fails.
the symmetric ball is fragmented into a limiting tiny-cube construction with near-zero almost-displacement energy, showing measure-theoretic rigidity fails
The fragmented near-displacement is a limiting arbitrarily-small-energy construction used to defeat a measure-theoretic rigidity intuition.
The tiny-piece almost-displacement of a ball is used to refute measure-theoretic rigidity, with energy and overlap driven to the zero limiting regime.
A symmetric ball is cut into tiny pieces to achieve arbitrarily small almost-displacement energy, refuting measure-theoretic rigidity.

examples.name: Projective space with constant Rawnsley function
examples.note: Complex projective space is mentioned as a familiar Kähler case where the Rawnsley density in the coherent-state integral formula is simply constant.
examples.engagement_reason: Projective space/CP^N is cited as a good case where the Rawnsley function is constant and calculable, but no calculation or construction is carried out on it.

passes.reason: Complex projective space is a homogeneous highly symmetric Kähler model where the Rawnsley function is constant, used as a standard example.

examples.name: Toy quantum proof of ball displacement energy
examples.note: The speaker presents the small ball at radius about sqrt(hbar) as the model that originally suggested that ideal semiclassical remainders would yield Hofer-type rigidity from the quantum speed limit, and then explains that the failure of the argument reveals necessary lower bounds on remainders.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs a toy argument for a ball of radius sqrt(hbar), quantizes a localized state, applies dislocation and the quantum speed limit, and derives the claimed lower bound before explaining the flaw.

passes.reason: The standard symplectic ball is symmetric, and the toy argument is used as an enemy case exposing why ideal small quantization remainders cannot hold.
The example centers on a round symplectic ball, hence symmetric, and is a joking/motivating proof of a true lower bound rather than a counterexample.
The symmetric ball in the toy argument is used to expose the false hope that quantization remainders stay negligible at the quantum scale.

The toy Planck-scale ball/coherent-state argument sits at the limiting quantum localization scale and is used to expose the obstruction caused by large quantization remainders rather than as a valid proof.
the toy argument uses the Euclidean ball as the symmetric standard domain to illustrate a lower-bound proof rather than to refute a claim
The toy argument uses a round ball at the quantum-cell/minimum-uncertainty scale in a heuristic proof, not as a refutation of displacement energy.
The quantum-cell-scale ball/coherent-state argument is a deliberately failed limiting construction used to obstruct ideal small-remainder quantization.
The toy argument uses a Planck-scale ball/coherent state at a boundary quantum scale to illustrate a heuristic proof, not to refute the theorem.
the symmetric quantum-cell-scale ball/coherent state is a minimal-uncertainty boundary case used as an enemy test for ideal Toeplitz remainders
The quantum-scale symmetric ball/coherent-cell test case sits at the uncertainty-scale boundary and is used as an obstruction to overly ideal quantization remainders.
The toy argument uses a symmetric quantum-scale ball/coherent state at the minimal uncertainty scale to illustrate a proof idea, not to contradict the displacement bound.
The Planck-scale symmetric ball/coherent-state argument is used to expose the failure of ideal small-remainder quantization.
